coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis and characterization of fluorescent nanoprobe based on unintentional heteroatom doped-carbon quantum dots for bioimaging of human neuroblastoma cancer cells and living organisms
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrated an economical, ecologically sustainable, and simple method for producing biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) co-doped with N, K, and Ca from bathua (Chenopodium album), a popular medicinal herb leaf extract and nutritious vegetable. The nearly spherical shaped CQDs were discovered to be 3–5 nm in size in nature, with a quantum yield of around 48.12%, robust photo-stability, and excellent water solubility. Various morphological, compositional, physicochemical and optical characteristics were investigated. Additional factors affecting the photoluminescence of CQDs, such as hydrothermal process parameters, pH, and solvent sensitivity, were investigated further. Moreover, the newly synthesized N, K, Ca-doped CQDs were demonstrated to be a useful fluorescent nanoprobe for in vitro cellular imaging of SH-SY5Y human neuroblastoma cells, as well as other living organisms such as bacteria, fungal, and plant cells, as a cost-effective alternative to organic or synthetic fluorescent probes. Cell survival and cytotoxicity were found to be acceptable, with 95% of SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 cells surviving and other organisms able to withstand higher doses (10.9 mg/mL) for up to 4 days.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrated an economical, ecologically sustainable, and simple method for producing biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) co-doped with N, K, and Ca from bathua (Chenopodium album), a popular medicinal herb leaf extract and nutritious vegetable. The nearly spherical shaped CQDs were discovered to be 3–5 nm in size in nature, with a quantum yield of around 48.12%, robust photo-stability, and excellent water solubility. Various morphological, compositional, physicochemical and optical characteristics were investigated. Additional factors affecting the photoluminescence of CQDs, such as hydrothermal process parameters, pH, and solvent sensitivity, were investigated further. Moreover, the newly synthesized N, K, Ca-doped CQDs were demonstrated to be a useful fluorescent nanoprobe for in vitro cellular imaging of SH-SY5Y human neuroblastoma cells, as well as other living organisms such as bacteria, fungal, and plant cells, as a cost-effective alternative to organic or synthetic fluorescent probes. Cell survival and cytotoxicity were found to be acceptable, with 95% of SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 cells surviving and other organisms able to withstand higher doses (10.9 mg/mL) for up to 4 days.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioimaging is one of the most effective ways to visualize living cells using fluorescence microscopy in various fields of biomedicine and clinical research [1,2]. This allows monitoring of biological functions in real time. Bioimaging studies often require a fluorescent probe or specific biomarker to visualize the cells/species in the sample [3]. Imaging is critical in modern biomedicine for identifying the structure and function of cells, detecting biomarkers in diagnosis, following the progression of present illness, treating different cancers, and monitoring treatment efficacy. Currently, the most common fluorescent nanomaterials are: semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), rare-earth up-conversion materials or noble metal nanoparticles [4]. Fluorescent nanomaterials have the benefits of high quantum yield, high stability, wide Stokes shift, broad excitation spectrum, and narrow emission spectrum when compared to conventional dyes. Their emission wavelengths may be varied by adjusting their size [5]. Their biocompatibility, recognition, and sensing properties may all be enhanced by assembly and modification. Overall, nanomaterials hold great promise for fluorescent labelling as well as the future of fluorescence imaging.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of nanocarbon that is now superior to semiconductor quantum dots due to its solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, ease of synthesis, and excellent luminescent properties. In addition, the production process can affect their physicochemical properties. CQDs are capable of emitting light in the ultraviolet and near infrared, making them ideal for biological applications [6–8]. CQDs are widely used as prospective imaging agents for in vitro and in vivo bioimaging due to their unique properties of intense fluorescence, superior photostability, high water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [9–11].

However, semiconductor quantum dots have several disadvantages, including increased toxicity due to the use of heavy metals in their manufacture. Unlike heavy metals, which can be very dangerous even at low concentrations, CQDs have minimal toxicity, a hydrophilic surface, easy passivation, stable fluorescence, resistance to photobleaching, and excellent biocompatibility. non-toxic and durable substitutes with desired properties for environmental and biological safety. Although various organic and inorganic fluorophores such as rhodamine 6G, dapi, hoechist-33258, hoechist-33342, acridine orange, ethidium bromide, hydroxycoumarin, Alexa fluor 488, Cy5, etc. are commercially available with a very high [80% quantum yield, but the use of organic fluorophores in bioimaging has significant disadvantages, such as susceptibility to photocorrosion and ease of leakage into the cytoplasmic environment [7–8]. They are also cytotoxic and have a limited wavelength range for excitation and emission.

Carbon quantum dots have become promising candidates for use in such important areas, which are a cost-effective alternative in this situation. Therefore, it is important to design and develop a simple, large-scale and environmentally friendly approach based on inexpensive starting materials. Pure biomaterial resources are often preferred over alternative organic, inorganic, or synthetic materials in the search for an efficient, inexpensive, and environmentally appropriate CQD synthesis method. The reason for this is mainly the biocompatibility and sustainability of biomaterials and their ability to turn biomass waste into a valuable commodity. Another advantage of using natural biomaterials is that, since they contain minerals, the synthesized CQDs can be doped together or functionalized without using additional resources. On the other hand, green synthesis methods are much more acceptable than traditional physical and chemical methods. Bottom-up hydrothermal technology using natural raw materials has attracted much attention due to its environmental friendliness, applicability, low cost, and mass production.

One of the main objectives of this research is to develop a simple and practical experimental technique for the production of low-cost CQDs based on the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of bathua (Chenopodium album) leaves-extract, a traditional medicinal plant and a nutritional vegetable. Chenopodium album has mild medicinal properties and is a nutritious and useful addition to the diet. It also has anthelmintic, anti-inflammatory, antirheumatic, contraceptive, laxative, odontalgic and other therapeutic effects [12].

In this study, we demonstrated the key materials and optical properties of N, K, Ca doped-CQDs synthesized from Chenopodium album leaves extract and their potential use as fluorescent markers in optical bioimaging of cancer cells and other living organisms.

Chenopodium album (Bathua) was gathered the nearby agricultural field of the University campus (GPS locations: 30o15’4”N, 77o2’39”E) for the synthesis of natural carbon quantum dots. Fresh lime juice was extracted for functionalizing the natural carbon quantum dots. Additional chemicals such as hexane, toluene, chloroform, acetone, ethanol, methanol, propanol, acetonitrile etc. were brought from Sisco Research Laboratory Pvt. Ltd. India. MTT (3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5diphenyltetra-zolium bromide) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. In this experiment, only analytical-grade chemicals were employed. Without any further purification, all substances were utilized exactly as they were received. Standard aqua regia solution (HCl: HNO3 in 3:1) was used to clean the glasswares. We used double-distilled deionized water (18.2 MΩ.cm−1) to make all of our hydrophilic solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Chenopodium album
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were obtained by a one-step hydrothermal process using the vegetable plant Chenopodium album as the main precursor for carbon. The Chenopodium album vegetable plant is washed with distilled water before its leaves are ground into juice using a household blender. In a glass beaker, 7 mL of plant extract was combined with 3 mL of freshly produced lemon juice for surface functionalization, then sonicated for 25 min.

The solution was then placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave. This synthesis was carried out for 45 to 720 min at temperatures ranging from 110 to 200 0C. After cooling to room temperature, the as-synthesised solution was centrifuged for 20 min at 8000 rpm. After centrifugation, the pollutants settled and the resultant solution was recovered. A 220 nm (polytetrafluoroethylene membrane) syringe filter was used to filter the produced supernatant solution in order to remove any impurities that centrifugation had not been able to completely remove. The purified solution was then lyophilized in a lyophilizer at −40 0C before being stored for later use. The pH of the as-synthesized CQDs solution was around 3.86. Fig. 1 depicts the entire method graphically.

The effect of shifting the pH of the medium from acidic to basic on the synthesis of N,K,Ca-doped CQDs was also investigated. The pH of the N,K,Ca-doped CQDs solution was adjusted either with NaOH or HCl solution before centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min to eliminate precipitate. The pH of the N,K,Ca-doped CQDs solution was maintained between 2 and 12 using a glass electrode pH sensor. The pH of the as-synthesized CQDs was observed to be around 3.86. The pH dependent characterstics of CQDs were examined by dropping CQDs solution into beakers containing NaOH or HCl solution with pH values ranging from 2 to 12.

The solvent polarity dependence of N,K,Ca-doped CQDs was studied in the presence of various solvent polarities ranging from low polarity solvents (such as hexane, toluene, and chloroform) to high polarity solvents (such as DI water, ethanol, and methanol). The as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs solution was then mixed with a 1:1 vol ratio of various solvents (hexane, toluene, chloroform, propanol, acetone, ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, and deionized water) and stirred for 5 min. Following that, excitation dependent luminescence was performed.

The morphological analysis of the CQDs was investigated by using high resolution transmission electron microscopy and selected area electron diffraction patterns. The high-resolution TEM images were taken on JEOL JEM 2100 plus with 200 kV accelerating voltage. The relative proportion of carbon and oxygen in as-synthesized CQDs was assessed using Energy-Dispersive x-ray spectroscopy on the JEOL JEM-2100F. Shimadzu IRSpirit FTIR was used to do the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic study, with a resolution of 1 cm−1 and scanning wavelength ranges of 400 to 4000 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction (D8 DISCOVER Bruker) method was used to analyse the crystal structure and crystallinity of the CQDs using Ni-filtered CuKα
1 radiation. The scanning was performed from 2θ = 10 to 800 with a step of 0.020 at 30 kV. Using a SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer, the UV–Visible spectroscopy of CQDs was recorded in the wavelength range of 230–750 nm with a step of 1 nm. SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer was used to perform the fluorescence spectroscopic examination on CQDs with excitation wavelengths ranging from 250 to 750 nm. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Physical Electronics PHI 5000 Versa Probe III, ranging from 0 to 1200 eV, the binding energies and elemental composition were examined. A 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer was used to perform the nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic (1H NMR and 13C NMR) for experimental studies. Using an Anton Paar Litesizer 500, surface Zeta potential was analysed. The REMI R8C or CPR-30 Plus centrifuge was used to centrifugation of the samples.

The relative quantum yield values (ɸ) for quinine sulphate dye, used as a reference standard material, for Chenopodium album plant derived CQDs were evaluated. The measurement details can be found from our previous publication [13]. The quantum yield obtained was around 48.12%.

The human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cell line and the human colon cancer HCT-116 cell line were obtained from the National Centre for Cell Studies in Pune, India. Confluent cell lines were carefully cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, streptomycin, and penicillin (1%), in a controlled carbon dioxide environment (5%), at 37° C. The used medium was discarded after the dead cells and debris were removed with a phosphate buffered saline wash. The cells were trypsinized using 0.25% trypsin. After another centrifugation, this time at 2500 rpm for 5 min, a pellet was formed. Fresh aliquots were prepared and transferred to fresh culture plates for sub-culturing.

To investigate the effects of N,K,Ca-doped CQDs, ∼104 SH-SY5Y cells were seeded onto a 12 well plate and incubated for 24 h. After 24 h, the cells were washed with 1X PBS and stained for 30 min with 100 µg/mL N,K,Ca-doped CQDs. The pH of the cells was adjusted to roughly 7.4 before staining with CQDs. The cells were then rinsed twice. After that, the cells were incubated for 30 min. The sample photos were taken with a fluorescent microscope (Carl Zeiss, Axio Vert A1, Germany).

In-vitro biocompatibility study was conducted using the cell lines SH-SY5Y and HCT-116. The viability test was performed three times. A 96-well plate with ∼104 cells per well was seeded with cells prior to the test. The cells were then treated the next day with CQDs diluted in medium at various doses to test their biocompatibility and cytotoxicity. CQDs were not used on the cells in the control group. The cells were washed after 24 h, and cell viability was determined using the MTT assay. The following formula was used to determine cell viability: CV % = [(A control - A sample)/ A control] × 100, Acontrol and Asample respectively are the absorbance of the untreated and treated cultures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Staining procedure of bioimaging of living organisms
ce:section.ce:para: We used a variety of materials for biological imaging in this study, including pine (Pinus roxburghii) plant leaves, mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus), fungus (Mucor circinelloides), and bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus). Sharp blades were utilized to slice enough pine leaves and mushrooms for slide processing. The cut portions float in the DI water. They are gently removed with a delicate brush and placed in a watch glass filled with DI water. Thin slices are taken using a brush, put on a slide in a drop of water, and viewed under an optical microscope. The dye is applied to a thin, even slice of skin. Following that, fluorescent stains are exclusively used to stain the surface of leaves and mushrooms. Because of this, the piece was first put up on a glass slide. The section slices were thoroughly stained with a produced N,K,Ca doped-CQD-based replacement fluorescent stain from Chenopodium album (0.2 mg/mL), then covered with a coverslip and studied under a fluorescence microscope. Similarly, fluorescent N,K,Ca-doped CQDs (0.2 mg/mL) were put on a glass slide, followed by a tuft of fungal mycelium spores, a coverslip, and the slide was inspected under a fluorescence microscope. Before air drying, the fluorescent N,K,Ca-doped CQDs were utilized to stain the bacterial smear. A sliding rack was used to hold the smears in place. After that, the fluorescent dye was carefully applied to the smear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity study in living organisms
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic fluorescence of CQDs was utilized to investigate the biocompatibility of a variety of materials, including pine leaves, mushrooms, fungi, and bacteria. During a four-day period, the feasibility study was conducted at various concentrations. Pine and mushroom epidermal layers were put on a glass slide prior to the study. The epidermal layers of pine and mushroom were then treated for four days with varying quantities of generated N,K,Ca-doped CQDs. Similarly, we tested the survivability of fungus and bacteria for 4 days at various doses (∼0.2 mg/mL, ∼1 mg/mL, ∼5 mg/mL, and ∼10 mg/mL).

High resolution-TEM analysis was used to examine the morphological properties of the as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs The HRTEM micrographs and SAED patterns are displayed in Fig. 2 (a)-(e). The as-synthesized CQDs are small but poorly distributed due to the absence of a passivation layer, and they are roughly spherical in shape, according to the TEM micrographs (Fig. 2(a)–2(c)). The crystalline characteristics of the as-synthesized CQDs was revealed from the HR-TEM images as can be seen from Fig. 2(d). The fast fourier transform (FFT) was used to calculate the lattice spacing. The measured lattice spacing of 0.266 nm, 0.271 nm, 0.275 nm [14,15]. As shown in Fig. 2(a) (inset), the as-synthesized N,K,Ca doped-CQD sizes are primarily scattered in the 3–5 nm range, with an average diameter of 3.75 nm. It is obvious that the distributions are bimodal. The explanation for this, we believe, is because quantum dots have a roughly spherical shape and their thickness is determined by the number of graphite layers.

Indeed, the diameter distribution and thickness (number of graphite layers) distribution are superimposed to create the TEM size distributions. Since the distribution of the N,K,Ca-doped CQDs on the TEM grid is affected, this raises the variability. Furthermore, the major diffused ring in the SAED pattern revealed the weak poly-crystalline structure of the as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs (Fig. 2(e)). The N,K,Ca-doped CQDs were analysed using EDX spectroscopy to determine their chemical makeup. The presence of high carbon (at. percent 54.86) and oxygen (at. percent 33.68) concentrations, as well as tracer amounts of nitrogen (at. percent 3.24), potassium (at. percent 3.02), and calcium (at. percent 2.32) concentrations in the EDX spectra shown in Fig. 2(f), suggests that the as-synthesized CQDs are effectively doped with N, K, and Ca. The presence of distinct functionalization groups is indicated by the higher oxygen concentrations seen on the surface of CQDs (such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, or carboxyl groups).

The optical properties of heteroatom doped-CQDs, according to several publications in the literature, are dependent on the chemical structure, such as surface functionalization, the amount of doping elements, and the location of dopant atoms (whether they are within the carbon core or on the surface) [16,17]. The chemical structure and composition of CQDs were further identified by XPS. The presence of C, O, N, K, and Ca in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 (a)) was identified at binding energies of 284.6, 531.6, 399.1, 291.9, and 26.2 eV, respectively. The atomic percentages of C, O, N, K and Ca were assessed to be approximately 61.43, 22.4, 7.56, 6.57 and 2.02%, respectively, which is consistent with the EDX study. Furthermore, high resolution C 1s and K 2p spectra were deconvoluted into five peaks appeared at 284.4, 285.9, 287.6, 291.9 and 294.7 eV, originating from sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, C-OH,
K
2 p
3 / 2
and
K
2 p
1 / 2
, respectively (see Fig. 3(b)) [18,19]. Similarly, the O1s spectra in Fig. 3c is found to be constituted of two components with centres at 531.1 and 532.9 eV, confirming the existence of the CO and C-OH groups [20,21]. The high-resolution N1s spectra displayed in Fig. 3(d) can be deconvoluted into two peaks, 397.1 and 399.4 eV, indicating graphitic-N and amine groups, respectively, revealing that nitrogen atoms were efficiently doped in CQDs [22]. Previous research has suggested that graphitic N increases the electronic cloud density of CQD surfaces, resulting in an increase in luminescence efficiency. Similarly, Ca 3p peaks (Fig. 3(e)) occurred at 26.2 eV, suggesting effective Ca doping in CQDs [23]. The inexpensive and environmentally friendly synthesis of CQDs from the Chenopodium album was therefore successful in adding N, K, and Ca without the need for other sources of doping elements, according to the XPS analysis. Overall, the resulting CQDs featured a number of O- and N-related functional groups that were not only responsible for their high water solubility, but might also be linked to their fluorescence emission.

The crystallinity of the as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs was verified by XRD, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). In the hexagonal graphite structure of CQDs, the (002) plane was attributed to the interlayer packing of disordered carbon atoms, producing a turbostratic structure [24,25]. The wide peak is assumed to be caused by the amorphous nature of the CQDs, which are apparently derived from sp3 carbon atoms. Meanwhile, the presence of steeper diffraction peaks at 19.4°, 25.2°, 43° and 76.4° correlates to the degree of graphitization along the (010), (004), (100) and (110) planes (JCPDS Card No. 26-1080) [26]. However, the observed small peak at 32.7° is appearing from the Ca (110) plane. Thus XRD indicates that efficient N,K,Ca doping enhances the crystalline nature of otherwise amorphous dominated CQDs. HRTEM examination revealed a significant degree of polycrystalline nature as well (see Fig. 2(c)).

NMR spectroscopy, on the other hand, was employed to investigate sp3-and sp2-compositions of CQDs. Carbon QDs have been shown to mix with sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms. 13C NMR was used to identify the sp3 and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. In fact, 13C NMR spectra in Fig. 4(b), is evident that there is a mixture of sp3 and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, with sp3-hybridized carbon atoms predominating.

Two distinct chemical shift (δ) zones were found, one between 60 and 80 parts per million (for sp3 carbons and carbons related to hydroxyl and amine groups), and the other between 190 and 200 parts per million (for carbons linked with hydroxyl and amine groups). As for the 1H NMR spectra, they show three distinct chemical shift (δ) regions: 1–3 parts per million (primarily provided by sp3 C-H protons), 3–6 ppm (associated with hydroxyl group protons), and 8–8.5 ppm (associated with aromatic or sp2 protons) [27]. Fig. 4(d) shows how FTIR studies revealed the presence of several functional groups and interactions in Chenopodium album derived carbon quantum dots. Stretching frequencies of 2359.72, 2300.59, 2190.16, 2142.42, 2048.37, 2009.19, 1981.40, 1721 and 1635.14 cm−1, respectively, significantly confirmed the existence of OCO, CC, CCNH, CCC, CO and COOH [28]. The presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surfaces of CQDs is revealed by all of the foregoing studies. Because of the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups, as-synthesized CQDs become highly soluble in water.

Using a zetasizer, the zeta potential, also called as the electro kinetic potential of as-synthesised N,K,Ca-doped CQDs, was measured to obtain the surface charge. This approach also reveals CQD characteristics, such as double layer qualities with a range of hydrophilic or hydrophobic functional groups. The stability of CQDs particles over short or extended time periods determines the value of zeta potential. As shown in Fig. 4(e), a zeta potential of −17.75 mV was found, suggesting that in aqueous solution, CQDs are incredibly soluble.

The implications of processing parameters including temperature and time on as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs were investigated further. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), eventually higher wavelengths were driven away from the threshold absorption peaks, 424.5 nm for 110 °C to 453.6 nm for 200 °C process temperature, while the process duration for all was kept constant at 3 h. This is due to the conjugated C = C band's normal π–π* transition and the graphitic core's n–π* transitions [29,30].

Meanwhile, the traditional Tauc plot was utilized to assess the fluctuation in the optical energy band gap, which ranged from 3.224 to 3.052 eV while the hydrothermal process temperatures ranged from 110 to 200 °C. The computation's specifics are described in the supplemental material (Fig. S1). Process time variation demonstrated a similar pattern from 45 min to 720 min while keeping the process's temperature steady at 200 °C, as shown in Fig. 5(b). Adequate agglomeration/nucleation is likely to occur for a longer period of time, leading particle size to rise and, as a result, absorption peaks to shift to higher wavelengths. Furthermore, the standard Tauc plot was utilised to assess the fluctuation in optical energy band gap owing to time dependent process variations ranging from 3.43 to 3.21 eV for 45 and 720 min process times, as shown in the supplemental data (see Fig. S2). However, the optimal hydrothermal process conditions were 200 °C and 3 h based on consistency and reproducibility, UV–Vis and fluorescence properties.

Carbon quantum dots exhibit size-dependent photoluminescence (PL) activity and emission wavelength. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission that is procedure parameter dependent of N,K,Ca-doped CQDs was examined. Fig. 6 (a) depicts the photoluminescence spectra of CQDs dissolved in water.

It was discovered that the PL emission spectra of CQDs were hydrothermally process-dependent. At lower process temperatures, the PL emission spectra showed an asymmetric peak range that was larger, spanning 359.3–455.8 nm, but as temperature is raised from 110 to 200 °C, it became sharper and more intense. The normalised PL characteristics given in Fig. 6(b) demonstrates this even more clearly. The deconvoluted fluorescence spectra, depicted in supplemental Fig. S3 as well as in Table S1, indicated three separate wide peaks for process temperatures in the range between 110 and 200 °C. Process temperature is rising from 110 to 200 °C, the primary peak migrated from its centre at a lower wavelength, 461 nm (Area contribution: 53.80 %, FWHM: 61.3 nm) to 443 nm (area contribution: 82.93 %, FWHM: 106.6 nm), while the middle peak moved from its centre to a higher wavelength, 353 nm (area contribution: 33.56 %, FWHM: 52.3 nm) to 515 nm (area contribution: 13.49 %, FWHM: 147.1 nm). Meanwhile, the shoulder peaks migrated upward from 508.9 nm (area contribution: 12.62%, FWHM: 59.4 nm) to 344 nm (area contribution: 3.57%, FWHM: 43.5 nm). As shown in Fig. 6(a) and an additional Fig. S3, all of the peaks dropped to lower wavelengths when the process temperature was increased to 200 °C. The asymmetric widening of the PL spectra was also visible in the normalised PL spectra, as shown in Fig. 6(b). The temperature-dependent sharper and more intense PL spectra show the multi-colour fluorescence emission, which is considered to be caused by an increase in the degree of carbonization and shape distortion of CQDs as process temperature rises. Similarly, the effect of process duration on PL emission was investigated for a given process duration. When the process time was minimal, the PL emission spectra widened asymmetrically after being illuminated with a 280 nm wavelength, but sharpened and intensified when the process time was extended to 12 h, as shown in Fig. 7 (a) and the normalised PL spectra in Fig. 7(b).

Again, for varied process durations, the deconvoluted fluorescence spectra, as shown in supplemental Fig. S4 (a)-(e) and Table S2, revealed three separate broad peaks. As the process duration increased from 45 min to 720 mins, (i) the primary contributing peak widened and moved to slightly higher wavelength from 455 nm (area contribution: 54.80 percent, FWHM: 93.30 nm) to 456 nm (area contribution: 57.66 percent, FWHM: 83.7 nm), (ii) The medium-contributed peak was enhanced the wavelength from 356 nm (area contribution: 23.27 percent, FWHM: 63.99 nm) to 489 nm (area contribution: 33.14 percent, FWHM: 143.75 nm), but the (iii) lowest contributed peak was moved to a shorter wavelength from 498 nm (area contribution: 21.92 percent, FWHM: 153.39) to 357 nm (area contribution: 09.18 percent, FWHM: 61.78 nm).

In order to achieve consistency and repeatability as well as optimal UV–Vis and fluorescence properties, the hydrothermal process parameters were optimised at 200 °C for 3 h. In fact, greenish-coloured fluorescence emission predominates PL emission investigations. The aqueous solution of CQDs fluoresces intensely and vividly green when exposed to UV light (365 nm, 60 W), as seen in Fig. 7(c). Furthermore, at room temperature, the aqueous solution of N,K,Ca-doped CQDs displayed a long-term homogenous phase with no evident precipitation. These findings demonstrate that the synthesised N,K,Ca-doped CQDs possess excellent fluorescence and water dispersibility.

Regardless of the environment or the organism cells, the pH value is an important metric. The fluorescence behaviour was studied at various pH levels, and the heteroatom doped-CQDs performed well in terms of pH sensitivity. The fluorescence intensity reduced significantly with decreasing pH values after 4, as demonstrated by the PL spectra in Fig. 8 a and its normalised plot in Fig. 8(b). However, when the pH was above 7.0 or below 5, the fluorescence intensity remained steady.

The deconvoluted spectra shown in supplementary Fig. S5 (a)-(k) and Table S3 show that each pH dependent PL spectra can be deconvoluted with three different peaks where the major peak remains almost unchanged (460 to 454 nm) for change in pH value from 2 to 6, but shifts towards 453 nm when pH becomes 7 but again shifts dramatically towards shorter wavelength (451 nm) when pH becomes strong basic 12. Fig. 8(c) depicts the observable colour variations under UV light for varied pH samples. The pH sensitivity of the PL of N,K,Ca-doped CQDs may result from the reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups like carboxylic and amide groups. This process might change the nature and speed of transition processes, which would then affect the PL spectrum and fluorophores' intensity [31].

The hypothesis of general solvent effects is useful for understanding solvent-dependent spectrum changes. The CQDs are a dipole in a continuous media with a consistent dielectric constant when discussing general solvent effects. The energy difference between the ground and excited states, as well as the orientation polarizability of solvents, are affected by interactions between the solvent and CQDs.

The electron distribution in a molecule with a certain structure is represented by the dipole moment. The dipole moment, in conjunction with the reactive field around it, is critical in a molecule's transition because a molecule may absorb light when its dipole moment changes. The as-synthesised N,K,Ca-doped CQDs PL emission spectra with dielectric constant and refractive index in a range of solvents were captured [32,33]. The solvent was chosen based on the polarity parameters, which encompass a wide range of solvent polarity and hydrogen bonding with the CQDs. Fig. 9 (a) depicts the PL spectra of Chenopodium album-derived CQDs dispersed in a variety of polar and non-polar solvents including hexane, toluene, chloroform, propanol, acetone, ethanol, acetonitrile, and DI water. PL intensity decreases for non-polar solvents as compared to polar solvents for the same excitation wavelength (280 nm). Again, all of the PL spectra may be deconvoluted into three wide peaks, the details of which can be seen in supplemental Fig. S6 and Table S4. As seen in Fig. 9 (b), the predominant PL peak location remained nearly constant between 454 and 453 nm. The as-synthesized CQDs have excellent solubility in polar solvents such as propanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, and water, but have low dispersibility in non-polar solvents such as toluene, hexene, and chloroform. After half an hour, CQD precipitation was also seen in this solution. Fig. 9(c) depicts digital pictures of produced CQDs dispersion in various solvents under day and UV light. Under UV excitation, there is no apparent change in visible yellow-greenish color. Hence, the as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs had no substantial surface modification and solvent dependent solvatochromism.

Scientific research on luminescent nanoparticles, which have numerous uses in biosensing, bioimaging, lighting, and display techniques, has never ceased to be quite active. Those with wavelength-controllable emissions, in particular, might be quite useful. This led to a further investigation into excitation-dependent PL emission. As shown in Fig. 10 (a) and its normalised plot in Fig. 10(b), when the excitation wavelength was increased from 250 to 535 nm in steps of 15 nm, the emission wavelength increased from 380 to 589 nm.

The quantum confinement effect, often known as the size effect, is one of the most widely accepted theories for how the excitation-dependent PL observed in experiments is produced [34–36]. Particularly, the shapes and sizes of the particles in CQDs determine their energy gaps; a smaller size results in a larger energy gap. A divergence in the energy gaps induced by the size distribution results in enlarged emission wavelengths in the 380–589 nm range. Additionally, different heteroatoms (unintentionally doped N, K, and Ca) are likely to play the role of multiple fluorescence centres in excitation dependent PL emission. These surface traps include –COOH, –OH, and C-O-C at the shell or dangling bonds at the surface or oxygen-related disorder-induced localised states [37–39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Chenopodium album derived N,K,Ca-doped CQDs
Cell imaging of human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells and cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro cellular imaging with N,K,Ca-doped CQDs on SH-SY5Y cells (human neuroblastoma cells) was explored evaluating the bioimaging and cell labelling properties of CQDs under a fluorescent microscope. SH-SY5Y cells were treated with 100 μg/mL N,K,Ca-doped CQDs for the duration specified in the experimental section before being prepared for fluorescence microscopy detection. SH-SY5Y cells exhibited intense greenish fluorescence that was equally dispersed throughout the cell as a consequence (Fig. 11 (a)-(r)). More interestingly, a low CQDs concentration of 100 μg/mL was sufficient to label SH-SY5Y cells with greenish fluorescence, indicating the potential use of CQDs in cell imaging. As has been previously noted, carbon nanoparticles typically emit significantly in the blue-light area whereas their long-wavelength emissions are frequently mild. The biological imaging and analytical uses of carbon nanoparticles with short-wavelength emissions are severely constrained by the fact that biological tissues often exhibited blue auto-fluorescence and were vulnerable to photo damage under UV light [40,41]. The as-prepared CQDs showed vivid greenish fluorescence when excited by light between 400 and 450 nm. Using N,K,Ca-doped CQDs for deep-seated cell identification may be possible thanks to the stimulated greenish fluorescence.

In addition to luminescence, outstanding biocompatibility and minimum toxicity are usually necessary when developing CQDs as a viable bio-labelling reagent. At acceptable concentrations, biological materials must be biocompatible [42,43]. To examine cytotoxicity of human neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) and colon cancer cells (HCT-116), they were treated for 24 h with as-prepared N,K,Ca-doped CQDs at final concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μg/mL. As demonstrated in Fig. 12 , MTT-assay experiments indicated that CQDs were essentially non-toxic, with more than 95 percent of cells surviving. These findings suggest that the newly synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs from Chenopodium album with excited greenish fluorescence have low cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, which bodes well for their prospective usage in biological imaging.

N,K,Ca-doped CQDs obtained from Chenopodium album were employed as fluorescent tags in the bioimaging of diverse plant foliage, mushrooms, fungi, and bacteria, as illustrated in Fig. 13 . The white button mushroom was used in this investigation (Agaricus bisporus). Pine leaves (Pinus roxburghii), fungus (Mucor circinelloides), and bacterium (Staphylococcus aureus) were also employed in bioimaging investigations.

The pine leaf structure is readily visible after staining in Fig. 13(a)-(d), with a mono-layered, tough outer epidermis covered by a thick cuticle and interrupted by surface depression stomata, amphistomatic. Each stoma has two guard cells and two subsidiary cells. It has apertures into a sub-stomatal cavity on the inside and openings into a vestibule cavity on the outside. A few layers of thick, sclerenchymatous hypodermis lie under the epidermis. There is no distinction between palisade and spongy parenchyma. The cells in this region are skinny, parenchymatous, polygonal, and densely packed, with chloroplasts and starch grains. The inner surface has peg-like infoldings. A single layer of endoderm with barrel-shaped cells and casparian strips surrounds it. In a multi-layered pericycle directly under the endodermis, a T-shaped cluster of sclerenchymatous cells is wedged between two vascular bundles [44].

Mucor circinelloides is one of the most common fungus that causes deadly mucormycosis. It is found all across the planet, primarily in soil, manure, and root crops. Colonies grow quickly, turning cottony to fluffy, white to yellow, then dark-grey as sporangia matures. Sporangiophores are upright, simple, or branching plants that produce huge (60–300 μm in diameter), terminal, globose to spherical, multispored sporangia with well-developed subtending columellae [45]. On the slide, cottony colonies are readily apparent as a bright color region with elongated sporangiophores made of larger spherical sporangia as illustrated in Fig. 13(e)-(h).

Meanwhile, a mushroom is made up of a stalk, a crown, and gills. The gills of a mushroom are often inspected under a microscope to determine its classification [46]. The spores are the most significant microscopic trait for identifying mushrooms. Mushroom spores are frequently detected by their color, shape, size, attachment, and sensitivity to chemical testing. After successfully staining with as-synthesized CQDs, the margins of the gills are clearly evident under the fluorescent microscope, forming the hymenial layer, or hymenium, which creates the spore-bearing structures known as basidia (see Fig. 13(i)-(l)). Many spores (basidiospores) can be seen on the surface of this layer or freely between the gills.

Staphylococcus aureus bacteria have a spherical form. They are most commonly seen on the mucous membranes of the mouth and respiratory system, where they have been associated to a variety of diseases and infections such as sepsis, pneumonia, and pharyngitis [47]. Staphylococcus aureus bacteria, like many others, are incredibly minute, ranging in size from 0.5 to 2.0 µm. Due of the low magnification of a fluorescent microscope, S. aureus shows a flashing of fluorescent green color emission (see Fig. 13(m)-(p)).

In contrast, the toxicity and survival rate of pine leaf, mushrooms, fungus, and bacteria have been examined, as shown in Fig. 14 . A cell viability of more than 80 % demonstrates that the CQDs are biocompatible and cause minimal cytotoxicity [48]. Fig. 14(a) and (c) demonstrate that N,K,Ca-doped CQDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity for pine leaf and mushroom, respectively, even at higher doses (10 mg/mL) for up to 4 days. Likewise, fungi and bacteria exhibited significant cytotoxicity for CQD dosages up to 0.2 mg/mL when tested for 4 days, as shown in Fig. 14(b)-(d).

It is important to note that carbon quantum dots' high emission quantum yield is still a hurdle and is required for their use in bioimaging applications. Additionally, the fluorescent nature of carbon quantum dots is significantly influenced by their flaws, necessitating increased bioimaging sensitivity or selectivity.

In summary, fluorescent carbon quantum dots doped with N, K, and Ca were synthesized in a fast, simple, and cost-effective manner using a hydrothermal assisted synthesis procedure from the herbal traditional medicine's leaf extract and nutritious vegetable known as bathua (Chenopodium album) as a green supply. The as-produced carbon dots demonstrated outstanding photostability, strong fluorescence intensity, and excellent yellow-greenish fluorescent emission with up to 48.12% quantum yield after UV exposure. According to HRTEM and XRD data, CQDs are amorphous and polycrystalline in nature with an average size of 4.75 nm and a shape close to spherical. Meanwhile, NMR spectra showed significant amounts of sp2 and sp3 carbon groups. In addition, the synthesized CQDs have a high water suspension capacity and Zeta potential tests show a negative surface charge (−17.75 mV). The influence of hydrothermal process parameters were also optimized. The hydrothermal process parameters were optimized at 200 °C for three hours based on consistency and repeatability, as well as excellent UV–Vis and fluorescence characteristics. Besides, CQDs were found to exhibit pH and solvent sensitive photoluminescence characteristics. In fact, a minor blue shift in pH-responsive PL spectra was found, which was most likely caused by reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups such as carboxylic and amide groups. CQDs were observed to be highly soluble in polar solvents such as propanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, and water, but have very poor dispersibility in nonpolar solvents such as toluene, hexane, and chloroform. However, the predominant PL peak location remained nearly constant between 454 and 453 nm except intensity variation. Furthermore, as a replacement for organic or synthetic dyes, as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs demonstrated remarkable fluorescent staining capabilities and could be used as a useful probe for in vitro cellular imaging of various tumours or living organisms. SH-SY5Y (human neuroblastoma cells), HCT-116 (human colon cancer cells), and other living species such as pine leaf, mushrooms, algae, fungus, and bacteria, on the other hand, have been investigated for bioimaging, cell cytotoxicity and cell viability. Cell survival and cytotoxicity have been found to be reasonable, with 95% of SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 cells surviving, and other live organisms able to resist larger dosages (10.9 mg/mL) for up to 4 days.

Vishal Kansay: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Varun Dutt Sharma: Methodology, Investigation. G. Chandan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Indu Sharma: Investigation. Anita Bhatia: Visualization. S. Chakrabarti: Supervision, Methodology. M.K. Bera: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
